bibliographic_data.title: Enhancing Eye Feature Estimation from Event Data Streams through Adaptive Inference State Space Modeling
bibliographic_data.abstract: Eye feature extraction from event-based data streams can be performed efficiently and with low energy consumption, offering great utility to real-world eye tracking pipelines. However, few eye feature extractors are designed to handle sudden changes in event density caused by the changes between gaze behaviors that vary in their kinematics, leading to degraded prediction performance. In this work, we address this problem by introducing the adaptive inference state space model (AISSM), a novel architecture for feature extraction that is capable of dynamically adjusting the relative weight placed on current versus recent information. This relative weighting is determined via estimates of the signal-to-noise ratio and event density produced by a complementary dynamic confidence network. Lastly, we craft and evaluate a novel learning technique that improves training efficiency. Experimental results demonstrate that the AISSM system outperforms state-of-the-art models for event-based eye feature extraction.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper introduces an adaptive inference state space model (AISSM) that dynamically adjusts the relative weight between current and recent information to improve eye feature extraction from event-based data streams.
core_content.key_methodology: The AISSM uses a dynamic confidence network to estimate signal-to-noise ratio and event density, which are used to adjust the weighting of current versus historical information. A novel long-horizon training technique is also introduced to improve model efficiency.
outcomes: The AISSM system outperforms state-of-the-art models for event-based eye feature extraction.
Dynamic confidence network aids in learning from dynamic behavior.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Eye tracking using frame-based cameras has limitations such as high power consumption and motion blur. Event sensors, or Dynamic Vision Sensors (DVS), offer advantages like higher frequency, lower latency, and lower power consumption. However, sudden changes in event density due to varying gaze behaviors can degrade prediction performance. This paper proposes the Adaptive Inference State Space Model (AISSM) which dynamically adjusts the weight between current and recent information based on signal-to-noise ratio estimates from a dynamic confidence network. The AISSM is evaluated against state-of-the-art models, demonstrating superior performance.
